changeReason: Improved translation from Japanese. Old: "Festival's Around the Corner"

TextENGNew: *murmur* *murmur* *murmur*...
changeReason: Old translation uses 'rabble' (a disorderly crowd), but ざわざわ is an onomatopoeia for murmuring or ambient noise, which 'murmur' captures more accurately.

TextENGNew: ``Hey, hey! Are you guys worn out already? The real thing is tomorrow!@k@vS20/00/442500002. Tomorrow night, we’re going all the way—burning bright until the wee hours, got it?!``
``Yes, sir! I’m fired up and ready!``
``Wahaha! That’s the spirit! Youth is everything!``
``Good work today, everyone! Watanagashi is finally tomorrow.@k@vS20/00/KeijiB44002. Get some rest tonight and save your energy!``
Tomorrow was finally Watanagashi.
Festivals have two sides: those who enjoy them, and those who make them happen.
The former need only prepare their spirits, but the latter need not only heart and body, but also meticulous planning and preparation.
While the carefree general public enjoys the festival, we’ll be maintaining our vigilance throughout.@k...What for?
``That’s obvious! Because the beer after it’s all over is the best damn beer you’ll ever taste!``
``Wahahahahaha!!``
At times like these, there’s nothing like the spirit of a sports team!
changeReason: Old translation says 'energetic, physically fit people,' but the Japanese 体育会系のノリ specifically means 'sports team spirit/mentality,' a particular Japanese cultural concept about athletic club camaraderie.

TextENGNew: You have to push through with the afterparty as motivation—otherwise, what’s the point!
changeReason: Old translation omits the key motivator 打ち上げ (afterparty); the Japanese says you push through by looking forward to the afterparty.

TextENGNew: ``Oh, he’s here...!``
Ooishi-san walked in.@kEveryone snapped to attention.
``Hello, hello, everyone. Good work out there. Ah, at ease.``
``````Good work, sir!!``````
Even my usually boisterous seniors couldn’t hold a candle to Ooishi-san.
We all bowed as if we were an old-fashioned cheering squad.
Kuraudo Ooishi.
A detective just running out the clock until retirement—one who largely ignores internal regulations and drifts through his days without any particular accomplishments.
He has the image of a vulgar old man with a taste for dirty jokes,@kbut according to my seniors, in his younger days he was quite the brawler, with a legendary track record... or so they say.
...Anyway.@kOne thing was certain: he had far more presence and gravitas than the division head who walked in with him.
``Everyone, I truly appreciate your hard work preparing for tomorrow’s Watanagashi.``
Everyone stood at attention, listening to the division head’s briefing.

``As occurred last year, there is a strong possibility of thrill-seeking criminals attempting to exploit the situation. All personnel are to maintain the utmost vigilance and fulfill their duties to prevent any crimes.``
``Of course, it would be best if nothing happened at all. ...But everyone.@k@vS20/11/440700114. Operate under the assumption that an incident will occur.``

``Prevention is our top priority, but—well—it probably won’t be enough. This year too, someone will die, and someone will vanish.@k@vS20/11/440700116. I’d stake my career on it.@k@vS20/11/440700117. ...Nfufufufu!``
Everyone except the division head cracked a wry smile.
``Ooishi-san... that won’t do! We need to take this more seriously...``
``The important thing isn’t simply getting through the night of the festival. ...It’s being able to swiftly, thoroughly, and relentlessly pursue whatever happens tomorrow night.``
changeReason: Old translation uses redundant 'quickly, swiftly' and 'aggressively,' while the Japanese specifies 迅速かつ徹底的にどこまでも (swiftly, thoroughly, relentlessly)--'thoroughly' is a distinct meaning from 'aggressively.'

TextENGNew: ``Give me everything you’ve got! Tighten those helmet straps!!@k@vS20/11/440700120. We’re going to rip the mask right off of Oyashiro-sama’s so-called curse!!!``
changeReason: Old translation says 'Clench those cheeks,' but the Japanese カブトの緒引き締めてけ means 'Tighten those helmet straps'--a samurai idiom for staying alert, completely different from the old translation.

TextENGNew: ``````YEAAAAAHHH!!!``````
